Accipitridae: powerful bare legs ending in strong grasping feet with thick curved talons; legs often yellow
Acrocephalidae: strong legs with long toes for gripping vertical reed stems; legs usually pale pinkish to grey
Aegithalidae: tiny slender dark legs and small delicate perching feet
Alaudidae: slender pale legs with a notably long, nearly straight hind claw
Alcedinidae: very short legs and small red or dark feet, the front toes partly fused (syndactyl)
Alcidae: short legs set far back on the body, with webbed feet; stands upright
Anatidae: short legs set fairly far back, with fully webbed front toes; legs and feet often orange, grey or black
Apodidae: tiny very short legs and small feet with all four toes pointing forward — clings, cannot perch upright
Ardeidae: very long bare legs and long slender unwebbed toes for wading; legs yellow, green or dark
Bombycillidae: short dark legs and strong little perching feet
Burhinidae: long, stout pale yellowish legs with thickened joints and only three toes (no hind toe)
Cacatuidae: short grey zygodactyl feet (two toes forward, two back), used to grasp and climb
Calcariidae: slender legs with a long hind claw
Caprimulgidae: very small short legs and tiny weak feet, often barely visible
Certhiidae: slender legs with long, strongly curved claws for clinging to bark
Charadriidae: medium-length legs and compact feet, usually with no hind toe (plover-type feet)
Ciconiidae: very long bare legs and long toes for wading; legs often red or dark
Cinclidae: strong sturdy legs and stout feet for gripping wet rocks in streams
Cisticolidae: slender pinkish legs and fine perching feet
Corvidae: strong, robust dark legs and large sturdy perching feet
Columbidae: short reddish or pink legs with typical perching feet
Cuculidae: zygodactyl feet with two toes pointing forward and two back
Emberizidae: slender pinkish-brown legs and fine perching feet
Estrildidae: small slender pinkish legs and delicate perching feet
Falconidae: strong bare legs, often yellow, with sharp curved talons
Fringillidae: short, fairly stout legs and small perching feet, usually pinkish to dark
Gaviidae: legs set far back on the body with large webbed feet; ungainly on land
Glareolidae: fairly short legs (pratincoles) to longer legs (coursers); slender toes
Haematopodidae: stout pinkish legs and feet with no hind toe
Hirundinidae: tiny short legs and small weak feet, used only for perching briefly
Hydrobatidae: long slender dark legs with small webbed feet that dangle in flight
Laniidae: strong dark legs and feet with sharp gripping claws
Laridae: fairly short legs with fully webbed feet; legs red, yellow, pink or black
Locustellidae: strong legs with long toes for creeping through dense grass
Meropidae: short legs and small feet with partly fused front toes (syndactyl)
Motacillidae: slender pale legs and fine feet; pipits have a long straight hind claw
Muscicapidae: slender dark legs and fine perching feet
Nectariniidae: slender dark legs and small perching feet
Oceanitidae: long slender dark legs with webbed feet that trail or patter on the water
Oriolidae: short bluish-grey legs and stout perching feet
Otididae: long, stout, bare legs with only three short thick toes, adapted for walking
Pandionidae: strong bare legs with powerful feet, curved talons and a reversible outer toe
Panuridae: slender legs with long toes for gripping reed stems
Paridae: slender dark legs and agile little perching feet
Passeridae: slender pinkish-brown legs and neat perching feet
Pelecanidae: short legs and large fully webbed feet joining all four toes (totipalmate)
Phalacrocoracidae: short legs set far back with large fully webbed feet joining all four toes (totipalmate)
Phasianidae: stout, strong legs and feet for walking and scratching the ground; males often spurred
Phoenicopteridae: extremely long bare legs and webbed feet for wading; legs pink
Phylloscopidae: fine, slender pale pinkish or brownish legs and small delicate perching feet
Picidae: short legs and strong zygodactyl feet (two toes forward, two back) with sharp claws for clinging to bark
Ploceidae: pinkish legs and stout perching feet
Podicipedidae: legs set far back on the body with broad lobed toes (flaps of skin, not full webs)
Procellariidae: legs set far back with webbed feet; awkward on land
Prunellidae: slender reddish-brown legs and fine perching feet
Psittacidae: zygodactyl feet (two toes forward, two back), often used to grasp food and climb
Psittaculidae: zygodactyl feet (two toes forward, two back), often used to grasp food and climb
Pteroclidae: very short legs with feathered toes; feet small
Pycnonotidae: slender dark legs and small perching feet
Rallidae: long legs and very long slender toes for walking on soft vegetation; coots have lobed toes
Recurvirostridae: extremely long, fine legs for wading; thin toes, slightly webbed
Regulidae: very fine, thin pale legs and tiny feet
Remizidae: slender legs and small perching feet
Scolopacidae: long, slender legs and unwebbed toes for wading and probing; leg colour varies by species
Sittidae: short, strong legs with large feet and long claws for climbing on bark and rock
Stercorariidae: fairly short legs with webbed feet; legs dark
Strigidae: feathered legs and feet ending in strong curved talons
Sturnidae: sturdy reddish-pink legs and strong perching feet
Sulidae: short legs set far back with fully webbed feet joining all four toes (totipalmate)
Sylviidae: slender legs and fine perching feet, usually grey or pinkish
Threskiornithidae: long bare legs and long toes for wading; toes slightly webbed at the base
Tichodromidae: strong feet with long curved claws for clinging to vertical rock faces
Troglodytidae: slender legs and strong little feet held in an upright, cocked stance
Turdidae: fairly long, slender pinkish or dark legs and neat perching feet
Tytonidae: long, lightly feathered legs ending in slender talons
Upupidae: short greyish legs and small feet
_default: legs and feet typical of the species, both clearly shown and not cut off
